Here's what actually happened: Libet had people press a button whenever they felt like it while monitoring their brain activity. Think of it like watching a wave build up offshore before it crashes on the beach - there's activity building before the dramatic moment. But here's the first logical issue: does unconscious brain activity really mean you have no free will, or does it just mean that consciousness isn't the whole story of what 'you' are? But there's a competing view called 'compatibilism' that says free will and determinism might both be true, depending on how we define 'free will.' But compatibilists say yes, because 'you' are the sum of those causes - your preferences, your reasoning, your values - so it's still your choice even if it was determined. **ALEX'S EMOTIONAL JOURNEY:**

**START:** Alex feels disconnected from family history. Their mom doesn't talk about the uncle, there's this hole in the family story, unanswered questions that make everything feel incomplete. Alex is frustrated by the silence, maybe a little resentful that nobody explains things.

**MIDDLE:** Through the investigation, Alex learns about courage and sacrifice. They see their uncle as a real person - flawed, scared, but trying to do the right thing even when it's dangerous. They have to make hard choices themselves, figure out who to trust, deal with consequences.

**END - varies by path:**

**Happy ending:** Alex learns that one person really can make a difference, that speaking up matters even when it's scary. They gain confidence in their own judgment.

**Bittersweet ending:** Alex understands that love sometimes means accepting painful choices. They learn that closure doesn't always mean happy endings, but understanding is its own kind of peace.

**Legacy ending:** Alex discovers that unfinished work can be completed, that they can honor someone by continuing what they started. They find purpose in carrying forward someone else's fight.

Every path shows growth - just different kinds. That's important.

I'd suggest 4-6 distinct endings to keep it manageable while still feeling meaningful. Here's how they could break down:

**POSSIBLE ENDINGS:**
• **Perfect Success** - They fix everything, timeline restored, they get home with new appreciation for their world
• **Partial Success** - Timeline mostly fixed but one small thing stays changed (bittersweet, teaches acceptance)
• **Personal Sacrifice** - They save the timeline but lose something personal, like a memory or object
• **Cautionary Ending** - If they make consistently selfish choices, things get worse and they're stuck
